skill.name: software-architecture
skill.description: Guide for quality focused software architecture. This skill should be used when users want to write code, design architecture, analyze code, in any case that relates to software development.
skill.summary: Quality-focused software architecture guide based on Clean Architecture and Domain-Driven Design principles
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All static analysis findings are false positives. Detected 'backtick execution' patterns are markdown code emphasis formatting for library names and file examples. 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' detections are references to 'Clean Architecture' design pattern, not cryptography. 'System reconnaissance' patterns match legitimate software development guidance. This skill contains no executable code and provides educational architecture guidance only.

content.value_statement: Stop reinventing the wheel with custom utilities and helpers. This skill teaches Clean Architecture and Domain-Driven Design principles to help you write maintainable, scalable code.
actual_capabilities: Provides guidance on Clean Architecture and DDD principles for structuring applications
Recommends domain-specific naming conventions to avoid generic anti-patterns like utils/helpers
Teaches library-first approach to reduce custom code maintenance burden
Guides separation of concerns between business logic, UI, and infrastructure layers
Offers code quality standards including early returns, function decomposition, and file size limits
limitations: Provides guidance only and does not execute code or modify files automatically
Requires user to implement recommended patterns and practices manually
Does not analyze existing codebases for architectural violations automatically
Principles are language-agnostic and may require adaptation for specific frameworks

use_cases.title: Greenfield Project Architecture
use_cases.description: Starting a new project and need to establish a solid architectural foundation from day one
use_cases.target_user: Senior developer or tech lead starting a new application

use_cases.title: Refactoring Legacy Code
use_cases.description: Improving code quality in an existing codebase by applying clean architecture principles
use_cases.target_user: Developer maintaining or modernizing legacy applications

use_cases.title: Code Review Standards
use_cases.description: Establishing consistent architectural guidelines for team code reviews and pull requests
use_cases.target_user: Engineering manager or team lead defining development standards

prompt_templates.title: Review My Code Structure
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this code component for architectural issues. Check for: generic naming (utils/helpers), business logic mixed with UI, file length over 200 lines, and functions over 50 lines. Suggest improvements following Clean Architecture principles.
prompt_templates.scenario: Getting architectural feedback on a specific component or module

prompt_templates.title: Design Module Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to build a [feature] module. Design the architecture following DDD principles. Suggest domain-specific names for entities, services, and repositories. Define clear boundaries and separation of concerns.
prompt_templates.scenario: Planning a new feature or module with proper architecture

prompt_templates.title: Find Better Library Solution
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to implement [feature] functionality. Search for existing libraries or services that solve this problem. Evaluate options based on: maintenance status, community adoption, documentation quality, and fit for my requirements.
prompt_templates.scenario: Researching third-party solutions before writing custom code

prompt_templates.title: Refactoring Anti-Patterns
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this codebase for architectural anti-patterns: NIH syndrome (custom implementations instead of libraries), generic file names, poor separation of concerns, and deep nesting. Provide specific refactoring recommendations with examples.
prompt_templates.scenario: Identifying and fixing architectural debt in existing code

output_examples.input: Review this UserService class for architectural issues: [ UserService class with database queries, API calls, and UI rendering all mixed together ]
output_examples.output: Architectural Issues Found: 1) Mixed concerns - database queries belong in Repository layer, 2) UI rendering should be in separate view component, 3) API calls should be in dedicated service. Recommended Refactor: Split into UserRepository (data), UserService (business logic), and UserView (presentation).

output_examples.input: I need to implement payment processing. Should I build my own or use a library?
output_examples.output: Recommendation: Use Stripe or Paddle instead of building custom payment processing. These services handle PCI compliance, recurring billing, webhooks, and edge cases that would take months to build yourself. Only consider custom implementation if you have highly unique requirements that existing solutions cannot meet.

output_examples.input: Design a module for order processing in an e-commerce app
output_examples.output: Recommended Architecture (DDD): 1) Order Entity (domain): Order, OrderItem, OrderStatus. 2) Repositories: OrderRepository, InventoryRepository. 3) Services: OrderService (placeOrder, cancelOrder), PaymentService (processPayment), InventoryService (reserveItems). 4) Anti-patterns to avoid: OrderController doing business logic, database queries in controllers, generic OrderUtils class.

best_practices: Search for existing libraries and services before writing custom code to reduce maintenance burden
Use domain-specific names like OrderCalculator and UserAuthenticator instead of generic utils or helpers
Keep business logic independent of frameworks and separate from UI components
Apply early return pattern to reduce nesting and improve code readability
anti_patterns: Creating utils.js or helpers.ts files with unrelated functions instead of domain-specific modules
Mixing business logic with UI components or putting database queries directly in controllers
Building custom authentication, state management, or form validation when established libraries exist
Naming files or modules generically (common, shared, misc) without clear domain purpose

faq.question: What is Clean Architecture?
faq.answer: Clean Architecture is a software design pattern that separates concerns into distinct layers: domain entities (business rules), use cases (application rules), interface adapters (controllers, presenters), and frameworks (UI, database). This independence makes your code testable and flexible.

faq.question: Why avoid utils and helpers files?
faq.answer: Generic utils and helpers become dumping grounds for unrelated code. They lack clear purpose and grow unmaintainable. Domain-specific names like OrderCalculator or EmailValidator communicate intent and keep code organized by business context.

faq.question: When should I write custom code instead of using a library?
faq.answer: Write custom code only for unique business logic, performance-critical paths with special needs, security-sensitive code requiring full control, or when existing solutions genuinely do not meet your requirements after thorough evaluation.

faq.question: What file size limits are recommended?
faq.answer: Keep functions under 50 lines when possible and files under 200 lines. Decompose longer components into smaller, focused pieces. If code cannot be reused elsewhere, keep it in the same file until splitting becomes necessary for maintainability.

faq.question: How does this skill differ from general coding assistants?
faq.answer: This skill focuses specifically on architectural decisions and code organization rather than syntax or implementation details. It guides high-level structure, design patterns, and maintainability rather than writing specific algorithms or fixing bugs.

faq.question: Can I use this skill for any programming language?
faq.answer: Yes. The architectural principles (Clean Architecture, DDD, separation of concerns) apply across all languages. However, implementation details vary between languages and frameworks, so adapt recommendations to your specific technology stack.
